skill.name: treatment-plans
skill.description: Generate concise (typically 1-4 pages) patient-centered medical treatment plans in LaTeX/PDF with SMART goals, evidence-based interventions, monitoring, and HIPAA-aware documentation.
skill.summary: Medical treatment plan generator that creates professional LaTeX/PDF documents with SMART goals, ICD-10 codes, and quality validation.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 409 static analysis findings were evaluated as false positives. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' flags correspond to LaTeX package includes (tcolorbox, tikz, xcolor) and regex-based text validation patterns in Python scripts, not actual cryptographic operations. 'System reconnaissance' and 'Network reconnaissance' flags match benign documentation content. 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' flags are bash command examples in markdown code blocks. The heuristic critical combination finding is based on these false positives and does not represent actual credential access or data exfiltration. The skill is a legitimate medical documentation tool with no confirmed security threats, though users should exercise caution with documented system commands (LaTeX package installation, compilation) and filesystem operations in the Python scripts.

medium_findings.title: sudo command documented in troubleshooting section
medium_findings.description: The STYLING_QUICK_REFERENCE.md includes an example sudo command (sudo tlmgr install) for installing LaTeX packages. While this is legitimate documentation for LaTeX setup, users should verify package sources before running privileged commands. This is a documentation example, not automated execution.

medium_findings.title: Shell commands documented in markdown code blocks
medium_findings.description: The SKILL.md, references/README.md, and STYLING_QUICK_REFERENCE.md contain numerous bash command examples (xelatex, bibtex, python, mkdir, cp, texhash) in code blocks. These are documentation examples for users to run manually. They are not automatically executed by the skill, but users should understand the commands before running them.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm flags - false positives from LaTeX packages
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged LaTeX package includes (tcolorbox, tikz, xcolor, geometry, hyperref, etc.) as weak cryptographic algorithm usage. These are standard LaTeX packages for document formatting and typesetting, not cryptographic operations. Regex patterns in Python validation scripts (icd10 code matching, SMART goal checking) were also flagged. All are false positives.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance flags - false positives from documentation
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged content in .tex templates and markdown documentation as system reconnaissance. The flagged lines contain medical content (goal descriptions, intervention guidelines, quality checklists). These are legitimate clinical documentation templates and reference materials.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL in SKILL.md - legitimate source reference
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a URL to the GitHub source repository (github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills). This is the legitimate source URL for the skill and is expected in open-source skill documentation.

low_findings.title: Ruby/shell backtick execution flags - false positives from markdown code blocks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged backtick-enclosed content in markdown files as Ruby/shell backtick execution. The flagged content is standard markdown code blocks showing bash command examples for LaTeX compilation and Python script execution. These are documentation examples, not actual code execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented sudo command for package installation
dangerous_patterns.description: STYLING_QUICK_REFERENCE.md documents a sudo tlmgr install command for installing LaTeX packages. While this is standard documentation practice for LaTeX setup, the presence of a sudo command in project documentation should be flagged as a dangerous pattern since it could encourage privilege escalation if users blindly execute commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem write operations in Python scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: Python scripts in the scripts/ directory perform filesystem operations: shutil.copy2 for template copying and file writes for output generation. These operations write to user-specified paths and could overwrite existing files if not carefully used.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell command execution instructions in documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documentation extensively documents shell commands (xelatex, pdflatex, bibtex, python, mkdir, cp, texhash) that users are expected to run manually. While these are legitimate LaTeX compilation and setup commands, the pattern of documented shell execution is flagged as a risk factor for users who may not understand the commands.

content.value_statement: Clinicians spend hours formatting treatment plans manually. This skill generates professional, HIPAA-aware medical treatment plans in LaTeX/PDF format with SMART goals, evidence-based interventions, and structured monitoring sections.
actual_capabilities: Generate 1-page quick reference treatment plans for busy clinical workflows
Create specialty-specific plans for chronic disease, mental health, rehabilitation, and pain management
Validate treatment plans against SMART goals criteria and clinical quality standards
Include evidence-based citations, ICD-10 codes, and monitoring parameters
Produce professional LaTeX/PDF output with color-coded sections and tables
Support multidisciplinary care coordination with clear roles and follow-up schedules
limitations: Cannot access patient medical records or integrate with EHR systems
Does not provide clinical decision support or drug interaction checking
Requires LaTeX distribution (TeX Live 2022+) for PDF compilation
Generated plans require clinician review and customization before use

use_cases.title: Busy Clinician Workflow
use_cases.description: A primary care physician needs to quickly generate a structured treatment plan for a diabetes patient during a 15-minute visit.

use_cases.title: Multidisciplinary Care Coordination
use_cases.description: A care coordinator needs a shared treatment plan that integrates inputs from specialists, therapists, and primary care providers.

use_cases.title: Clinical Education and Training
use_cases.description: A medical education director wants to teach residents how to write structured, evidence-based treatment plans with SMART goals.

prompt_templates.title: Simple One-Page Treatment Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a one-page treatment plan for a patient with type 2 diabetes. Include SMART goals, medication plan, monitoring schedule, and lifestyle recommendations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick clinical reference for a routine follow-up visit

prompt_templates.title: Chronic Disease Management Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a chronic disease management plan for a 65-year-old patient with congestive heart failure and COPD. Include ICD-10 codes, medication management, monitoring parameters, and escalation thresholds.
prompt_templates.scenario: Long-term care coordination for complex multimorbidity

prompt_templates.title: Mental Health Treatment Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a mental health treatment plan for a patient with treatment-resistant depression. Include pharmacotherapy plan, therapy frequency, suicide risk assessment, and safety plan.
prompt_templates.scenario: Behavioral health treatment with safety monitoring requirements

prompt_templates.title: Multidisciplinary Pain Management Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a comprehensive multidisciplinary pain management plan for a patient with chronic low back pain. Include multimodal analgesia, physical therapy protocol, behavioral health referral, and opioid risk mitigation strategy.
prompt_templates.scenario: Complex pain management requiring coordinated care across specialties

output_examples.input: Create a one-page treatment plan for a patient with hypertension.
output_examples.output: A single-page PDF document with patient information box, SMART goal boxes for blood pressure targets (under 130/80 mmHg within 3 months), medication table with ACE inhibitor dosing, lifestyle recommendations, quarterly monitoring schedule, and red-flag escalation criteria.

output_examples.input: Generate a chronic disease management plan for a diabetic patient with neuropathy.
output_examples.output: A 3-page document with front-page executive summary, ICD-10 coded diagnoses, SMART goals with HbA1c and blood pressure targets, medication management table, foot care protocol, quarterly monitoring schedule, and patient education checklist with red flags.

output_examples.input: Create a rehabilitation treatment plan for post-ACL reconstruction.
output_examples.output: A phased rehabilitation protocol with Phase 1 immobilization and quad sets (weeks 1-2), Phase 2 range of motion exercises (weeks 3-6), Phase 3 strengthening program (weeks 6-12), and return-to-sport criteria with functional testing milestones.

best_practices: Always de-identify patient information before sharing treatment plans outside the clinical team to maintain HIPAA compliance.
Use the 1-page quick reference format as the default for routine clinical visits to minimize documentation burden.
Validate generated plans with the included Python scripts to ensure completeness and SMART goal quality before sharing.
anti_patterns: Do not add excessive citations or bibliography sections. Use 0-3 brief in-text citations per plan as needed.
Do not include detailed pathophysiology or disease education sections. Focus on actionable treatment information.
Do not generate plans longer than 6 pages. Use the executive summary format on page 1 for scan-friendly clinical reference.

faq.question: What LaTeX distribution do I need to compile treatment plans?
faq.answer: You need TeX Live 2022+ or MiKTeX with support for xelatex or pdflatex engines. The style package requires tcolorbox, tikz, and fontspec packages.

faq.question: Is this skill HIPAA compliant?
faq.answer: The skill generates templates and documentation standards. You must de-identify all patient information before sharing treatment plans outside the clinical team.

faq.question: Can this skill integrate with my EHR system?
faq.answer: No. The skill produces standalone PDF files. EHR integration and direct data import are not supported.

faq.question: How many treatment plan formats are available?
faq.answer: Seven formats are available: one-page quick reference, general medical, mental health, rehabilitation, chronic disease management, perioperative care, and pain management.

faq.question: Does this skill provide clinical decision support?
faq.answer: No. Plans are templates that require clinician review and customization for each patient. The skill does not check drug interactions or provide treatment recommendations.

faq.question: Can I customize the LaTeX styling of the treatment plans?
faq.answer: Yes. The medical_treatment_plan.sty package supports color scheme customization, layout adjustments, and box style modifications.
